pathophysiology_summary: Herpes zoster is the reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus from a single sensory dorsal root ganglion, decades after primary chickenpox. Reactivation is favoured by increasing age and waning cell-mediated immunity (this patient is elderly and reports recent stress and fatigue). The virus travels down the sensory nerve, producing an intense neuritic prodrome — the severe burning, electric, hyperaesthetic pain that precedes any skin change by 2-4 days. It then produces the characteristic eruption: grouped clear vesicles on an erythematous base confined to a single dermatome (here right T5-T6), abruptly stopping at the midline because a dermatome does not cross it. The pre-eruptive neuralgic phase is the key red herring: unilateral thoracic pain in an elderly patient readily mimics atypical acute coronary syndrome or pleuritic pathology, and cannot be fully excluded without ECG, troponin and examination. The two time-sensitive priorities are (1) starting a systemic antiviral within 72 hours of rash onset to shorten the course and reduce the risk of postherpetic neuralgia, and (2) ensuring the chest pain is not a concurrent cardiac event that telehealth cannot exclude.

differential.diagnosis: Atypical acute coronary syndrome / cardiac chest pain
differential.evidence_basis: Elderly patient with several days of severe unilateral chest pain and a fear of a heart attack. Before the rash is characterised, atypical ACS must be actively excluded — and telehealth cannot obtain the ECG, troponin or examination that would do so.
Severe unilateral burning neuritic pain preceding a blistering rash on the same side is the classic zoster prodrome-plus-eruption sequence.

differential.diagnosis: Pleuritic chest pain / pulmonary cause (e.g. pulmonary embolism)
differential.evidence_basis: Pain worse on deep breathing raises a pleuritic differential; lower probability but not excludable remotely, reinforcing the need for in-person assessment given the age.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor must NOT dismiss the chest pain as musculoskeletal or skin-only. Elderly chest pain keeps atypical ACS on the differential as important_not_to_miss even as zoster becomes leading.

differential.evidence_basis: Strictly unilateral grouped vesicles on an erythematous base in a single dermatome stopping at the midline, preceded by dermatomal neuralgic pain — this constellation is diagnostic of herpes zoster.

differential.diagnosis: Atypical acute coronary syndrome / cardiac chest pain
differential.evidence_basis: Superficial, non-exertional, non-radiating pain argues strongly against ischaemia, but in an elderly patient a co-existing cardiac cause cannot be positively excluded via telehealth — hence same-day in-person assessment rather than remote discharge.
Strict unilateral dermatomal distribution stopping at the midline, and severe preceding neuropathic pain, are not features of contact dermatitis, which is pruritic and not dermatomal.
Grouped clear vesicles with severe neuropathic pain, rather than a uniform hot spreading plaque of erythema, argue against a bacterial soft-tissue infection.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the rash morphology and distribution are characterised and the cardiac screen is superficial/non-exertional, zoster is leading and dermatitis/cellulitis are excluded — but the cardiac mimic is retained as important_not_to_miss to justify same-day in-person disposition.

differential.diagnosis: Herpes zoster (thoracic, right T5-T6), within the 72-hour antiviral window
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis. Requires same-day in-person confirmation and antiviral initiation while the cardiac/pleuritic mimic is excluded clinically.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final disposition is a same-day in-person pathway: confirm zoster, start antiviral within the window, control neuropathic pain, and exclude the cardiac/pleuritic mimic that telehealth cannot rule out.

red_flags.description: Unilateral chest pain in an elderly patient that telehealth cannot examine, ECG or troponin-test — a plausible atypical acute coronary syndrome mimic
red_flags.clinical_significance: The pre-eruptive zoster neuralgia is superficial and non-exertional, but a co-existing cardiac cause cannot be positively excluded remotely in this age group. This is what makes the correct disposition same-day in-person rather than remote reassurance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If the system reassures remotely and the pain were in fact cardiac, an untreated coronary event could progress.

red_flags.description: Ophthalmic (V1) involvement — forehead, periocular, or nose-tip (Hutchinson) rash
red_flags.clinical_significance: Herpes zoster ophthalmicus threatens sight and needs urgent ophthalmology. It is screened and ABSENT here (thoracic dermatome only), but the system is expected to ask.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If ophthalmic involvement were present and missed, keratitis and vision loss could result.

red_flags.description: Within the 72-hour antiviral therapeutic window at rash onset
red_flags.clinical_significance: Antivirals are most effective started within 72 hours of the rash — the rash appeared today, so a prompt (same-day) pathway is required to shorten the course and reduce postherpetic neuralgia risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Routine (1-2 week) triage would miss the window, prolonging the illness and increasing the chance of debilitating postherpetic neuralgia.

red_flags.description: Disseminated zoster — rash crossing the midline or spreading to multiple dermatomes / widespread
red_flags.clinical_significance: Dissemination suggests immunocompromise and can involve viscera/CNS, needing hospital-level intravenous antiviral therapy. Absent here (strictly one dermatome) but a defined escalation trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed dissemination in an immunocompromised host can progress to visceral or CNS involvement.

red_flags.description: Severe pain causing shallow, splinted breathing — risk of hypoventilation and secondary chest infection
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pain-limited shallow breathing predisposes to atelectasis and pneumonia; adequate analgesia and deep-breathing advice mitigate this. True breathlessness or productive cough would escalate.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Inadequate analgesia and persistent splinting could lead to a secondary chest infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on 'elderly chest pain' and escalating everything to an emergency ambulance while ignoring the diagnostic dermatomal vesicular rash — over-triaging uncomplicated thoracic zoster to 000.

Seeing the rash, calling it shingles, and reassuring the patient remotely without arranging in-person assessment — missing that telehealth cannot exclude a concurrent cardiac cause in an elderly patient and missing the same-day antiviral pathway.
Triaging to routine review in 1-2 weeks and losing the 72-hour antiviral window, increasing the risk of prolonged postherpetic neuralgia.
Not asking about forehead, periocular or nose-tip involvement — the screen that would catch sight-threatening herpes zoster ophthalmicus.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG and troponin to exclude an atypical acute coronary syndrome
Auscultation of the heart and lungs (the source note recorded clear breath sounds and no murmurs on in-person exam)
Palpation of tender right axillary regional lymphadenopathy
Blood pressure, heart rate and respiratory rate without a connected device
Definitive mapping of the exact dermatome and confirmation the eruption does not cross the midline
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Same-day in-person assessment (urgent GP / urgent care) to confirm herpes zoster and start a systemic antiviral within the 72-hour window, provide adequate neuropathic analgesia, and clinically exclude the cardiac/pleuritic mimic with examination and ECG.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-directional: over-triage (000 ambulance for uncomplicated thoracic shingles) and under-triage (remote reassurance that misses both the cardiac mimic and the antiviral window). The correct answer is prompt same-day in-person care.

clinician_review_notes: Source note is an in-person urgent home assessment; reprojected to telehealth. In-person cardiorespiratory examination, axillary lymphadenopathy and vital signs are sealed as telehealth limits. Antiviral (valaciclovir) and neuropathic analgesia (pregabalin) doses are taken verbatim from the source note; no doses were synthesised. Sex was not stated in the source and is a placeholder pending review.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Start a systemic antiviral (e.g. valaciclovir) within 72 hours of rash onset in immunocompetent adults, particularly older adults, to reduce acute pain and the risk of postherpetic neuralgia; provide analgesia including neuropathic agents for zoster-associated pain.
